blocks.text: Chapter 3.
Jumping to Conclusions: WYSIATI and Substitution
The ideas in 30 seconds
System 1 is a machine for jumping to conclusions: it resolves ambiguity instantly, believes by default, and builds the most coherent story it can from whatever evidence happens to be available.
WYSIATI — "What You See Is All There Is" — means System 1 never asks what evidence is missing, which produces overconfidence, framing effects, and base-rate neglect.
The halo effect makes one known trait color everything else we infer about a person or idea; the cure is to decorrelate errors by collecting independent judgments before anyone shares an opinion.

System 1 continuously computes "basic assessments" (safe/dangerous, friend/foe, dominant/submissive) — so continuously that a tenth-of-a-second glance at a face predicted about 70% of U.S. Senate races.
When a question is hard, we silently answer an easier one instead — substitution — which is the common mechanism behind most judgment heuristics, including Slovic's affect heuristic.
Emotion often leads and "reasoning" follows: our likes and dislikes determine what we believe about risks and benefits, not the other way around.
The Machinery of Jumping to Conclusions

Jumping to conclusions is efficient when stakes are low and the conclusion is probably right; it is costly when the situation is unfamiliar, the stakes are high, and there is no time to gather more information. System 1 does it constantly and invisibly.

Consider "Ann approached the bank." You pictured a financial institution — unless the previous sentence mentioned a river. System 1 resolved the ambiguity using recent context, generated a single interpretation, and never told you an alternative existed. Doubt and ambiguity are System 2's business, and System 2 is often busy or lazy.

Believing works the same way. Following Spinoza rather than Descartes, psychologist Daniel Gilbert showed that understanding a statement begins with an attempt to believe it; "unbelieving" is a second operation that requires System 2. When people had to memorize digits while reading nonsense claims, they later remembered the false claims as true — a depleted System 2 could not do the unbelieving. Tired or distracted people are therefore more gullible, which is why persuasive ads run late at night.

Confirmation bias compounds this. Asked "Is Sam friendly?" you retrieve instances of friendliness; asked "Is Sam unfriendly?" you retrieve the opposite. System 1 tests hypotheses by searching for confirming evidence — a positive test strategy — where science demands attempts at refutation. Even System 2 tends to test ideas by looking for support rather than counterexamples.

The umbrella principle over all of this is exaggerated emotional coherence, better known as the halo effect: liking one thing about a person inclines you to like everything about them, including traits you have never observed. The tendency runs on the same associative coherence-seeking machinery — System 1 wants a simple, consistent story, and gaps in the evidence do not stop it.
The Halo Effect and How to Decorrelate Error

Solomon Asch demonstrated how sequence drives impressions. Alan is "intelligent, industrious, impulsive, critical, stubborn, envious"; Ben is the same list reversed. People like Alan far more. The first traits set the frame, and later traits are reinterpreted to fit: stubbornness in an intelligent person reads as principled; intelligence in an envious, stubborn person reads as dangerous. The order of evidence matters far more than it should, because early impressions suppress the meaning of later ones.

Kahneman caught the halo effect in his own grading. He used to read each student's exam booklet straight through, and a strong first essay lifted his scores on every essay that followed. When he switched to grading all answers to question one across every booklet, then all answers to question two, his scores became less coherent — the same student now got a mix of grades — and he felt less confident. The discomfort was informative: the new procedure was less pleasant but more accurate, because it stopped the first essay from contaminating the rest.

The general remedy is to decorrelate error. Independent judgments contain errors that partially cancel out; judgments made after discussion share the same errors and merely amplify confidence. This is why the wisdom of crowds works only when guesses are made independently — one loud early opinion in a jar-of-pennies contest destroys the aggregation.

The practical rule for meetings: before an issue is discussed, have every participant write a brief summary of their position. Open discussion gives disproportionate weight to whoever speaks first and most assertively; collecting views first harvests the full diversity of knowledge in the room instead of a chorus around the initial speaker.
WYSIATI: What You See Is All There Is

System 1's defining limitation is that it works only with currently activated ideas. Information it does not retrieve — because it was never presented, or is not associatively linked — might as well not exist. Kahneman's clumsy but memorable label: WYSIATI, "What You See Is All There Is." System 1 builds the best story possible from available evidence, and the story's coherence — not the evidence's quantity or quality — determines the confidence we feel. A good story built on flimsy, one-sided evidence often feels more compelling, because fewer facts means fewer contradictions.

The direct demonstration: subjects heard evidence from a legal dispute. Some heard only the plaintiff's side, some only the defense's, some both. One-sided subjects knew they were hearing one side, yet they were more confident in their judgments than people who heard both. Coherence beats completeness.
WYSIATI explains a family of biases developed later in the book:
Overconfidence. Confidence tracks the coherence of the story we can tell, not the amount or reliability of the evidence behind it. We rarely allow for the evidence we do not have (see Chapter 6).

Framing effects. "90% survival" and "10% mortality" are logically identical, but each frame activates different associations, and System 1 does not construct the alternative frame on its own (Chapter 8).

Base-rate neglect. A vivid description of a shy, tidy individual dominates the statistical fact that farmers vastly outnumber librarians, because the description is seen and the base rate is not (Chapter 4).
Judgments on Tap: Basic Assessments and the Mental Shotgun

System 1 is not idle between decisions. It continuously performs basic assessments of the situation — threat level, familiar or novel, friend or foe, dominance and trustworthiness — an evolutionary surveillance system that once determined survival and now determines elections.

Alex Todorov showed subjects pairs of candidates' faces for as little as a tenth of a second and asked which looked more competent. Those snap ratings — driven by cues like a strong chin and a slight confident smile — predicted the winners of about 70% of U.S. Senate, House, and gubernatorial races. Follow-up work found the effect is roughly three times larger among politically uninformed voters who watch a lot of television: the less real information System 2 has, the more the face-based basic assessment rules the vote.

Two other features of this automatic judgment machinery matter. First, System 1 represents categories by prototypes and averages, not sums. Shown a scattering of lines, people instantly judge the average length but cannot intuit the total length — that requires effortful System 2 computation. This blindness to sums underlies later puzzles like scope neglect and Hsee's less-is-more effect (Chapter 4).

Second, intensity matching: because System 1 represents intensity on a universal scale, we can effortlessly translate across unrelated dimensions — matching crimes to punishments, or answering "If Julie read fluently at age four, how tall is a man who is as tall as Julie was precocious?" This talent enables the badly non-regressive intuitive predictions dissected in Chapter 5.

Finally, the mental shotgun: we compute more than we intend. Asked whether statements are literally true, people are slower to reject false statements that rhyme or that are metaphorically apt — the brain assessed the rhyme and the metaphor even though the task never asked for them. Excess computation means irrelevant assessments constantly leak into our judgments.
Substitution: Answering an Easier Question

When System 1 faces a hard target question and no ready answer comes, it often swaps in a related, easier heuristic question, answers that, and lets intensity matching translate the answer back. This substitution is Kahneman's general definition of a heuristic. "How much would you contribute to save an endangered species?" becomes "How much emotion do I feel when I imagine dying dolphins?" "How popular will the president be in six months?" becomes "How popular is he now?" Crucially, you never notice the swap — the answer arrives with full subjective confidence, and a lazy System 2 typically endorses it.

The cleanest demonstration is the dating study by Strack, Martin, and Schwarz. German students asked "How happy are you these days?" and then "How many dates did you have last month?" showed essentially zero correlation between the answers. Reverse the order — dates first — and the correlation jumped to 0.66. The dating question primed a romance-tinged evaluation that then stood in for the global happiness question. The same order effect appears with finances, health, and family relations: whatever is emotionally salient at the moment of judgment becomes the answer to "How is your life?" This mechanism returns as the focusing illusion in Chapter 9.

The most consequential substitution is the affect heuristic, studied by Paul Slovic: people's beliefs about the world are determined by their likes and dislikes. "What do I think about nuclear power?" becomes "How do I feel about nuclear power?" In the real world, risks and benefits of technologies are largely independent; in people's heads they are strongly negatively correlated — things we like feel low-risk and high-benefit, things we dislike feel the reverse. Slovic showed that reading a persuasive case for a technology's benefits also lowered people's estimates of its risks, with no risk information provided at all. Emotion moved first; the factual beliefs fell into line behind it. System 2, in these moments, acts less like a critic and more like a lawyer defending System 1's verdict.
In practice
Before any group discussion, have every participant write down their independent judgment first — this decorrelates errors instead of amplifying the first confident voice.

When evaluating a person across several dimensions (hiring, grading, performance reviews), score each dimension separately across all candidates rather than judging one person whole, to block the halo effect.
When a conclusion feels obviously right, ask explicitly: "What evidence am I not seeing, and what would the other side say?" Confidence measures story coherence, not evidence quality.
Catch substitution in the act: when you answer a hard question instantly, name the easier question you probably answered instead ("Do I like this?" is rarely the question that was asked).
Treat any judgment made while emotionally aroused — enthusiasm or dread — as an affect-heuristic output, and deliberately seek risk and benefit information from independent sources.
Test your beliefs the way science does: hunt for the observation that would prove you wrong, not for one more example that fits.
